speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Your fasting blood glucose today is 9.2 mmol/L — that’s elevated and suggests your diabetes may not be well controlled.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, I’ve been feeling really thirsty lately and going to the bathroom much more often — especially at night.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, that’s the polyuria I mentioned — it’s been going on for about a week now.

speaker: doctor
text: Thank you for sharing that. Frequent urination like that can be a red flag for hyperglycemia. Have you noticed any blurred vision or fatigue?

speaker: doctor
text: I’m prescribing Lisinopril 10 mg once daily — we’ll use this to help protect your kidneys and manage blood pressure, given your glucose levels.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that right away. Should I take it in the morning or evening?

speaker: doctor
text: Morning is fine — with or without food. Let’s also check your hemoglobin while we’re here.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin came back at 151 g/L — that’s within the normal range for you.

speaker: patient
text: That’s reassuring. But I did check my blood sugar again at home this morning before coming in — it was 9.2 mmol/L, same as your reading.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure today is 131 mmHg — nicely within target. How’s your energy level been lately?

speaker: patient
text: Honestly, pretty low — and I’ve had some mild shortness of breath when climbing stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s repeat the hemoglobin test — I’d like to double-check because your symptoms suggest possible anemia or volume shifts.

speaker: patient
text: That number matches the reading you gave me earlier — 9.2 mmol/L — so it’s consistent, at least.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, the repeated glucose confirms persistent hyperglycemia. But your hemoglobin has dropped — it’s now 128 g/L, which is a meaningful decrease from the 151 we saw earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: That drop raises concern — could indicate early anemia, dehydration, or even medication-related effects. We’ll investigate further.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed my blood pressure feels higher — my home cuff showed 150 systolic this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Indeed — I just rechecked, and your systolic is now 150 mmHg, up from 131 earlier. That’s a notable rise and may relate to volume status or sympathetic activation.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the rising BP, falling hemoglobin, and persistent hyperglycemia, I’m going to switch you from Lisinopril to Telmisartan 40 mg daily — it offers better renal protection in diabetics and may improve volume tolerance.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — should I stop the Lisinopril today and start Telmisartan right away?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, discontinue Lisinopril and begin Telmisartan this evening. We’ll repeat blood work in one week — including CBC, creatinine, and HbA1c — and reassess your glucose logs.
